content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

February 23, 1994

Scott Kessler
Planning Director
Modoc County
P.O. Box 2083
Alturas, CA  96101

                                        Re:  Your Request for Advice
                                             Our File No. A-94-017

Dear Mr. Kessler:

     This letter will confirm the advice I provided to you on
December 27, 1993, concerning your obligations as Planning
Director for Modoc County pursuant to the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

     As we discussed, as a public official you are prohibited from
making, participating in making, or in any way attempting to use
your official position to influence a governmental decision in
which you know or have reason to know that you have a financial
interest.  (Section 87100.)

     An official has a financial interest in a decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate
family or on any economic interest, including any real property in
which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $1,000
or more.  (Section 87103(b).)


_____________________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA  95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-94-017
Page 2


     As I advised, since you own real property in the jurisdiction
that will be the subject of city decisions, you may not make,
participate in making, or in any way influence governmental
decisions which materially affect the property.  (See, In re
Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198; Regulation 18702.1.)  In addition,
where you are required to disqualify yourself from a decision, you
must disclose the financial interest as part of the agency's
official record or in writing to your supervisor.2

     As we discussed, participation in governmental decisions has
been interpreted broadly in furtherance of the goals of the Act.
Participation includes voting, making an appointment, committing
an agency to a course of action, entering into a contractual
agreement on behalf of the agency, determining not to act,
negotiating, advising on, or making recommendations to the decision-
maker.  In addition, a public official is prohibited from
contacting, appearing before, or otherwise attempting to influence
any member, officer, employee or consultant of the official's
agency concerning a governmental decision.  (Regulations 18700 and
18700.1.)

     Finally, please note that if you and your spouse were the
sole owners of the property in question, you could appear in the
same manner as any other member of the general public before your
agency in the course of its prescribed governmental function to
represent your own interest in real property.  (Regulation
18700.1(b)(1)(A).)

     However, according to your facts, you will own 40 acres of
the 177 acres that will be the subject of the decision.  Thus, the
exception would not apply.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        By: John W. Wallace
                                            Counsel, Legal Division


_________________________________________________________________

2    Pursuant to our telephone conversation you have submitted a
copy of the letter you provided to your agency regarding the
conflict of interest.  The letter will be retained with your
request for advice.  Our letter should not be construed as a
comment on the content of your disclosure letter.

sections.question_synthetic: What are the conflict-of-interest obligations for a Planning Director who owns real property in the jurisdiction that will be subject to governmental decisions, and can they participate in or influence those decisions?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: As Planning Director with ownership of 40 acres of 177 acres subject to a governmental decision, the official is prohibited from making, participating in, or influencing that decision due to financial interest. The official must disclose the conflict and may not appear before the agency regarding this matter, as the public participation exception only applies when the official and spouse are sole owners of the affected property.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No explicit QUESTION or CONCLUSION section headers, but content clearly shows this is an FPPC response letter (dated February 23, 1994) confirming prior telephone advice from December 27, 1993. The letter discusses conflict-of-interest law and specific application to the requester's situation regarding 40 acres of 177-acre parcel.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are the conflict-of-interest obligations for a Planning Director who owns real property in the jurisdiction that will be subject to governmental decisions, and can they participate in or influence those decisions?

CONCLUSION: As Planning Director with ownership of 40 acres of 177 acres subject to a governmental decision, the official is prohibited from making, participating in, or influencing that decision due to financial interest. The official must disclose the conflict and may not appear before the agency regarding this matter, as the public participation exception only applies when the official and spouse are sole owners of the affected property.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter confirming the Planning Director's conflict-of-interest obligations under the Political Reform Act regarding real property owned in their jurisdiction, clarifying that they cannot participate in or influence governmental decisions affecting that property and must disclose the financial interest.
